title: Handling a Storage Unit or Safe Deposit Box
summary: Storage units and safe deposit boxes sometimes hold important documents or items, and accessing them follows specific rules.
category: After a Loss III: Estate & Digital
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Handling a Storage Unit or Safe Deposit Box | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Storage units and safe deposit boxes sometimes hold important documents or items, and accessing them follows specific rules.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Safe deposit box access after death often requires proof of authority (executor documentation).
Some states allow limited access to search specifically for a will or burial instructions.
Storage unit access is generally simpler but still requires proof of authority.

paragraphs: Banks generally require documentation confirming legal authority (executor paperwork, sometimes a court order) before granting access to a safe deposit box, though some states allow a limited, supervised search specifically to look for a will or burial instructions before full authority is established.
Storage units are usually simpler to access, typically requiring proof of the renter's death and the requester's relationship or authority, though specific policies vary by facility.

nextSteps: Ask the bank about their specific safe deposit box access policy and any limited-search provisions.
Contact the storage facility directly about their access requirements.
Bring death certificate and identification to any access appointment.
disclaimer: General information — bank and storage facility policies vary and may also be affected by state law.

sources.name: FDIC: Five Things to Know About Safe Deposit Boxes, Home Safes and Your Valuables
